name: Balinese
description: Common phrases, caste-influenced vocabulary levels, and cultural terms tied to rituals and daily life in Bali.

entries.contextual: Casual-to-neutral 'yes'. Widely used in everyday agreement.
entries.culturalTip: This means 'yes' in casual context — respond with a smile and a nod. In formal settings, use 'inggih' instead.

entries.contextual: Polite 'yes' (basa alus). Used with elders, priests, or higher caste.
entries.culturalTip: Use this in temples or with respected elders. Combine with palms together (cakupan tangan) for deep respect.

entries.contextual: Polite thank you. 'Matur suksma' is the fuller, more respectful form.
entries.culturalTip: Reply 'mewali' (you're welcome). A slight bow with palms together makes gratitude feel sincere.

entries.contextual: The traditional Hindu Balinese greeting used at any time of day.
entries.culturalTip: Press your palms together at chest level (like a prayer) and bow slightly. Return the same greeting and gesture.

entries.contextual: A well-wishing word, as in 'rahajeng semeng' (good morning).
entries.culturalTip: Greet back warmly with the same phrase. Smiling is expected — Balinese greetings carry genuine warmth.

entries.contextual: Casual 'no worries / it's fine'. Very common reassurance slang.
entries.culturalTip: A relaxed, friendly phrase. Say it with an easy smile to put others at ease after a small mishap.

entries.contextual: The small palm-leaf offerings placed on the ground and shrines daily.
entries.culturalTip: Never step on or over a canang on the sidewalk — walk around it. Stepping on offerings is deeply disrespectful.

entries.contextual: Polite 'already' — e.g. already eaten, already finished.
entries.culturalTip: Often the polite answer to 'have you eaten?'. Replying 'sampun' even symbolically keeps the exchange courteous.

entries.contextual: The humble word for 'I' used in polite speech.
entries.culturalTip: Use 'tiang' instead of the casual 'icang' when speaking to elders or strangers to show humility.

entries.contextual: A gesture of deep respect with palms together raised toward the face.
entries.culturalTip: Reserved for prayer, priests, and sacred contexts. Don't use it casually — it carries strong spiritual meaning.
